name: Rutherfordium
name_origin: Named after New Zealand-born British physicist Ernest Rutherford, often called the 'father of nuclear physics'.

discovery.discoverer: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory team (Albert Ghiorso et al.) and Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Dubna

occurrence.abundance_summary: Rutherfordium does not occur naturally on Earth. It is a synthetic element produced in trace quantities by bombarding heavy actinide targets with light-ion beams in particle accelerators.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

compounds.name: Rutherfordium tetrachloride
compounds.summary: A volatile chloride characterized in gas-phase chromatography experiments; its volatility is comparable to ZrCl4 and HfCl4, supporting the placement of Rf in group 4.

reactivity_summary: Rutherfordium is predicted and experimentally indicated to behave as a group 4 transition metal, chemically similar to hafnium and zirconium, with a dominant +4 oxidation state. Relativistic effects on its 7s and 6d orbitals introduce subtle deviations from straightforward periodic-trend extrapolation.
isotopes_summary: All known isotopes of rutherfordium are radioactive. The most stable confirmed isotope is 267Rf with a half-life of about 1.3 hours; many shorter-lived isotopes (e.g., 261Rf, 263Rf) are used in chemistry experiments because they are easier to produce.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Rutherfordium
Schadel, M. 'Chemistry of Superheavy Elements', Angewandte Chemie Int. Ed., 2006
